summary: O'Neil highlights Paul Pfleiderer's concept of 'chameleon models' - theoretical models in finance and economics that shift between being presented as abstract theory and being used to make real-world policy claims. She notes that models aren't inherently harmful but become dangerous when weaponized politically. She partially pushes back on Milton Friedman's view that models should be judged by predictions rather than assumptions, arguing that stakes matter. She applies this framework to education policy, suggesting even flawed models like value-added teacher assessments could theoretically be used constructively.
key_points: Chameleon models are theoretical constructs that get misused by shifting between abstract theory and concrete policy justification
Whether to judge models by assumptions versus predictions depends on the stakes involved
Most models are harmless until deployed as political weapons
Even problematic models like value-added teacher assessments could theoretically serve constructive purposes like identifying schools needing support
Current education data is distorted by teaching to the test and cheating, making signals unreliable
money_quotes: Most models are harmless until they are used as political weapons.
The larger the stakes, the more the assumptions matter.

Even the value-added teacher model could be used to identify school systems that need support, although in the current climate of distorted data due to teaching to the test and cheating, I think the signal is probably very slight.
key_insight: Mathematical models become dangerous not through their construction but through their political deployment, and the higher the stakes, the more we must scrutinize their underlying assumptions.
